---
title: "Tanacross Alaska porphyry-Cu — the public-data template"
subtitle: "Same modeling pipeline, different region, different commodity, reproducible numbers"
date: 2026-05-11
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-fold: true
---

## The experiment

The Tanacross 1:250,000 quadrangle covers a porphyry-copper-
molybdenum belt in eastern Alaska that the USGS has flagged as a
tier-one critical-minerals target area. We built a porphyry-
prospectivity pipeline on this footprint using the same class of
public open data as Mother Lode but adapted to Alaskan geological
surveys.

The point of running Tanacross alongside Mother Lode was to test
whether the same modeling pipeline produces reproducible numbers on
a geologically and commodity-distinct region without per-region
retuning. Same model architecture, same cross-validation harness,
same evaluation metrics, only the input data and the deposit type
change.

## Data layers

### Composite view — what the AOI looks like

A combined visualization of DEM, geology overlay, and geophysics
overlay. The Tanacross AOI runs from the Yukon border west to the
Mentasta Mountains, with the porphyry belt along the Mid-Cretaceous
intrusive arc.

![Composite view: DEM + geology + geophysics overlay](../../_freeze/notebooks/eastak/data_exploration/figure-html/composite-view-output-1.png)

### Geochemistry — AGDB4 stream sediments

The Alaska Geochemical Database (version 4) carries pathfinder
elements for porphyry-Cu: Cu, Mo, Au, Ag, As, Sb. Mid-resolution
stream-sediment sampling at roughly one sample per 50 km².

![AGDB4 stream-sediment Cu, log10 scale — the headline pathfinder column for porphyry-Cu](../../_freeze/notebooks/eastak/data_exploration/figure-html/agdb4-cu-map-output-2.png)

![AGDB4 stream-sediment data, multi-element composite](../../_freeze/notebooks/eastak/data_exploration/figure-html/agdb4-map-output-1.png)

### Geology — USGS SGMC with Alaska overburden mask

State Geologic Map Compilation, restricted to the bedrock-exposed
fraction of the AOI (Quaternary overburden masked out — porphyry-Cu
exploration cannot see through unconsolidated cover).

![SGMC geology in the Tanacross AOI](../../_freeze/notebooks/eastak/data_exploration/figure-html/geology-map-output-1.png)

### Geophysics — USGS gridded aeromagnetic and Bouguer gravity

The basement-rock signature. Porphyry-Cu intrusive centers typically
produce a positive magnetic anomaly (from magnetite in the host
intrusion) and a moderate negative gravity anomaly (from the
intrusive's lower density relative to surrounding country rock).

![USGS magnetic + Bouguer gravity overlay](../../_freeze/notebooks/eastak/data_exploration/figure-html/geophys-output-1.png)

### Remote sensing — Sentinel-2 alteration proxies

Sentinel-2 spectral indices for iron oxide and hydroxyl-rich clay
alteration. Porphyry-Cu deposits typically produce a halo of
hematite/limonite plus argillic alteration at surface.

![Sentinel-2 RGB composite over the AOI](../../_freeze/notebooks/eastak/data_exploration/figure-html/s2-rgb-output-1.png)

![Sentinel-2 iron-oxide index (BSI proxy)](../../_freeze/notebooks/eastak/data_exploration/figure-html/s2-iron-oxide-output-1.png)

### Topography — DEM

Slope, aspect, and terrain ruggedness layers derived from a
1-arc-second DEM. Context features for the intrusive-belt geology.

![DEM in the Tanacross AOI](../../_freeze/notebooks/eastak/data_exploration/figure-html/dem-output-2.png)

## How the algorithm consumes the data

Same Random Forest plus 20-kilometer spatial-block cross-validation
harness as Mother Lode. The model builds an ensemble of decision
trees over the joint feature space; each tree learns conditional
rules like "prospective if (AGDB4 Cu above 50 ppm) AND (within 2 km
of an intrusive contact) AND (within 5 km of a mapped fault)." The
trees vote; the score is the proportion voting prospective.

The known porphyry-Cu positives in the AOI: 45 cells inside the
clean MRDS + Alaska Resource Data File label set, after the same
Cox-Singer commodity-and-deposit-type filter we used on Mother
Lode.

## Results

### Out-of-fold prospectivity map

The leak-free out-of-fold prospectivity raster across the AOI. The
high-prospectivity belt aligns with the published mid-Cretaceous
intrusive arc; the model has surfaced exactly the structurally-
controlled magmatic belt the regional geology would predict.

![Tanacross out-of-fold prospectivity raster](../../_freeze/notebooks/eastak/eastak_porphyry_prospectivity/figure-html/prospectivity-raster-output-1.png)

### Success-rate curve — top-k capture

The success-rate curve plots cumulative fraction of held-out
positives captured (y-axis) against cumulative fraction of area
flagged (x-axis). A perfect model would jump to 100 percent on the
y-axis with a tiny fraction of area on the x-axis. Random sampling
gives the diagonal line. Useful models sit well above the diagonal
in the lower-left corner.

![Tanacross success-rate curve. Top 1% captures 22.2% of held-out positives; top 5% captures 64%.](../../_freeze/notebooks/eastak/eastak_porphyry_prospectivity/figure-html/success-rate-output-2.png)

### SHAP feature attribution

The SHAP analysis ranks features by their average effect on model
output. The top features are AGDB4 Cu and Mo stream-sediment values
(the headline pathfinder columns), followed by the SGMC canonical
lithology one-hots for the porphyry-host intrusive units, then
distance to mapped fault traces.

![SHAP feature attribution: top features for the Tanacross porphyry-Cu model](../../_freeze/notebooks/eastak/eastak_porphyry_prospectivity/figure-html/shap-summary-bar-output-1.png)

## Interesting finding 1: same pipeline, different region, comparable numbers

The out-of-fold cross-validated **top 1 percent of cells captured
22.2 percent of held-out positives**, an enrichment of roughly 22 over
the density of deposits that random sampling would produce. **Top 5
percent of cells captured 64 percent of held-out positives.** The
absolute numbers are different from Mother Lode (different deposit
count, different feature stack, different geology), but the relative
performance is in the same regime: tree-based models on public open
data deliver useful exploration value at the regional scale.

This is the second independent region whose comparable numbers
support the methodology paper's reproducibility claim. When a
published method's performance is quoted on a single region, that
number alone tells you nothing about whether the method will work
on the next region. Running the same methodology on a second region
with a different commodity is the minimum bar for a portable claim.

## Interesting finding 2: feature engineering choices that worked, and one that did not

SHAP analysis on the Tanacross fit surfaced AGDB4 Cu and Mo
stream-sediment values as the top-ranked features, followed by the
SGMC canonical lithology one-hots for the porphyry-host intrusive
units, then distance to mapped fault traces.

The features that did **not** survive the cross-region transfer test
were the **count-style features** ("number of MRDS occurrences within
5 km") that we had initially included as a domain-knowledge proxy
for historical exploration density. Those features inflated
within-region AUC but did not transfer; they encoded "places people
have already looked" rather than "places where deposits actually
form." Removing them was a small within-region cost (a fraction of
a percentage point of AUC) for a substantial cross-region honesty
gain.

The lesson is that some features look helpful inside a region for
the same reason they fail outside it: they encode information about
the labeling process, not about the underlying geology. A feature
that says "many deposits have been found here historically" is a
proxy for "exploration money has been spent here," not a proxy for
"deposits exist." The two correlate inside a single region but
diverge across regions; the latter is the only signal that
generalizes.

## Files and reproduction

- Pipeline driver: [scripts/eastak/path2_stage1bc_oof_kenorland.py](https://github.com/00skyking00/ai-minerals/blob/main/scripts/eastak/path2_stage1bc_oof_kenorland.py)
- Region config: [src/ai_minerals/regions/eastak.py](https://github.com/00skyking00/ai-minerals/blob/main/src/ai_minerals/regions/eastak.py)
- Original Tanacross notebook: [notebooks/eastak/eastak_porphyry_prospectivity.qmd](../../notebooks/eastak/eastak_porphyry_prospectivity.qmd)
- Data exploration: [notebooks/eastak/data_exploration.qmd](../../notebooks/eastak/data_exploration.qmd)

## Sources

- USGS, gridded aeromagnetic data for Alaska.
- USGS, Bouguer gravity for Alaska.
- USGS, Alaska Resource Data File (ARDF) — historical occurrence records.
- USGS, State Geologic Map Compilation (SGMC) — Alaska coverage.
- Alaska Division of Geological and Geophysical Surveys, Alaska Geochemical Database version 4 (AGDB4).
- ESA Copernicus, Sentinel-2 multispectral imagery.
- USGS, 1 arc-second DEM (NED).
